summary: Engineering task plan for the 1985-NY-Yankees Azure Function: a nightly Timer-triggered Python 3.11 function that queries GPT-4o via TRAPI, validates the roster response, and persists structured JSON to dedicated Azure Blob Storage using Managed Identity for all service authentication. Covers infrastructure provisioning (Bicep), core application implementation, observability/alerting, security hardening, CI/CD pipeline, and release readiness.
assumptions: TRAPI supports Azure AD Managed Identity bearer token authentication; if not, a Key Vault secret fallback is required.
TRAPI endpoint, API version, and auth scope will be confirmed by rciapala before APP-02 begins.
Player scope is limited to the 25-man active roster (24–28 members); coaching staff and front-office are excluded unless PRJ-02 resolves otherwise.
TRAPI is accessible over the public internet; no VNet integration required for initial deployment.
Consumption Plan is acceptable; no strict cold-start SLA on the 2 AM trigger window.
Existing Bicep modules (storage, functionapp, rbac, monitoring) in infra/ are a starting point and will be extended, not replaced.
GitHub Actions OIDC federation to Azure is available for the deployment identity.
Staging deployment slot is supported on the target Consumption Plan SKU (requires at least Standard for slots; if not, a separate staging Function App is used).
Application Insights workspace-based instance is provisioned; 30-day retention and sampling are configured in Bicep.
host.json already exists in the repo and will be updated in-place.
open_questions: Does TRAPI support Azure AD Managed Identity bearer tokens, or is a client secret / API key required?
What is the TRAPI base URL, API version, and auth scope for GPT-4o access?
Should player scope include coaching staff, injured list, or front-office personnel in addition to active roster?
Is TRAPI network-restricted (requiring VNet integration and private endpoint for storage)?
Is there a strict SLA on the 2 AM execution window that would require Premium Plan with always-ready instances?
Does the target Azure subscription support deployment slots on the Consumption Plan, or is a separate staging Function App needed?

waves.name: Foundation
waves.objective: Establish all infrastructure, identity, and secret management prerequisites so Core Build work is unblocked.
entry_criteria: Azure subscription and resource group available
TRAPI auth mechanism and endpoint confirmed (PRJ-01 resolved)
exit_criteria: All Bicep modules deploy successfully to staging resource group
Function App Managed Identity exists with correct RBAC on storage container and Key Vault
TRAPI credentials (if any) stored in Key Vault and referenceable as App Settings
Application Insights workspace provisioned and connected to Function App
PRJ-01 and PRJ-02 resolved with documented decisions

waves.name: Core Build
waves.objective: Implement all functional requirements: TRAPI client, schema validation, blob writer, and Timer Trigger function.
entry_criteria: TRAPI endpoint and auth scope confirmed
exit_criteria: GetAndStoreYankeesRoster function executes locally via func start
TRAPI client returns valid roster JSON against staging/dev TRAPI endpoint
Blob writer writes date-stamped blob to yankees-roster container using Managed Identity
Conditional PUT prevents duplicate writes on same-day retrigger
Failed responses route to failed/ prefix
host.json updated with functionTimeout=120 and maxConcurrentCalls=1

waves.name: Hardening
waves.objective: Harden security, reliability, and observability; add alerting and test coverage.
entry_criteria: Core Build wave exit criteria met
Function executes end-to-end locally without errors
exit_criteria: No secrets or connection strings in source code (confirmed by secret scan)
All Key Vault references resolve correctly in staging App Settings
Structured logging emits all required key log events
player_count_returned custom metric emitted on each run
Application Insights alert rules provisioned (failure, duration, data quality)
Unit test suite passes with coverage for TRAPI client, schema validator, and blob writer
Integration test asserts Mattingly, Winfield, Henderson appear in blob output

waves.name: Release Readiness
waves.objective: Ship to production via a validated CI/CD pipeline with staging slot swap, active monitoring, and complete documentation.
entry_criteria: GitHub Actions deployment identity configured with OIDC federation
exit_criteria: GitHub Actions pipeline deploys to staging slot, runs smoke test, and swaps to production on push to main
End-to-end staging execution verified (timer trigger fires, blob written, no alerts triggered)
Production deployment successful and smoke test passing
All three alert rules active and notifying rciapala
README documents local dev setup, TRAPI auth instructions, and blob naming convention
All spec success criteria checked off

tasks.title: Confirm TRAPI auth mechanism, endpoint, and API version
tasks.description: Engage the TRAPI team to confirm whether Managed Identity bearer tokens are supported, obtain the base URL, API version, and auth scope for the GPT-4o endpoint. Document decisions in the repo as ADR or in spec.md Open Questions.
outputs: Confirmed TRAPI endpoint URL
Confirmed auth mechanism (Managed Identity scope or Key Vault secret)
API version string for prompt pinning
acceptance_criteria: TRAPI base URL and API version documented in repo
Auth mechanism confirmed: Managed Identity scope string OR Key Vault secret name recorded
Decision recorded in spec.md or ADR file
risks: TRAPI does not support Managed Identity, requiring Key Vault secret fallback and additional INF work

tasks.title: Confirm player scope definition for roster query
tasks.description: Resolve whether the roster includes only 25-man active roster members or also coaching staff, injured list, and front-office personnel. Decision gates prompt template authoring and validation threshold configuration.
outputs: Documented player scope decision
Validated player count range (currently 24–28)
acceptance_criteria: Player scope (active roster only vs. expanded) documented
Validation thresholds (min/max player count) confirmed and updated in spec if changed
Known anchor players list confirmed (Mattingly, Winfield, Henderson)
risks: Expanded scope changes validation thresholds and prompt template, delaying APP-01 and APP-03

tasks.title: Provision Azure Key Vault via Bicep
tasks.description: Add a Key Vault Bicep module to infra/modules/keyvault.bicep and wire it into main.bicep. Configure zone-redundant SKU, soft-delete enabled, and grant the Function App Managed Identity the Key Vault Secrets User role. Store TRAPI credentials (if not Managed Identity) as secrets.
outputs: infra/modules/keyvault.bicep
Updated infra/main.bicep with Key Vault module reference
Key Vault Secrets User RBAC assignment for Function App identity
acceptance_criteria: Key Vault provisions successfully in staging resource group
Soft-delete and purge protection enabled
Function App Managed Identity has Key Vault Secrets User role (scoped to Key Vault)
TRAPI credential secret (if applicable) stored and resolvable via Key Vault reference
az deployment group validate passes with no errors
risks: Key Vault reference resolution latency on cold start; ensure App Settings cache is warm

tasks.title: Provision dedicated Storage Account and yankees-roster container via Bicep
tasks.description: Extend or confirm infra/modules/storage.bicep to provision a Standard_LRS Cool-tier storage account with allowSharedKeyAccess:false, the yankees-roster container (private, no anonymous access), and soft-delete with 7-day retention. This is the dedicated data storage account, separate from the host storage account.
outputs: Updated infra/modules/storage.bicep with Cool tier, soft-delete 7-day, allowSharedKeyAccess:false
yankees-roster container with publicAccess:None
acceptance_criteria: Storage account provisions as Standard_LRS Cool tier
allowSharedKeyAccess is false
yankees-roster container exists with publicAccess:None
Blob soft-delete enabled with 7-day retention
Separate from Function App host storage account
risks: allowSharedKeyAccess:false requires identity-based auth; azure-storage-blob SDK must use DefaultAzureCredential

tasks.title: Provision Function App with Managed Identity and App Settings via Bicep
tasks.description: Extend infra/modules/functionapp.bicep to ensure system-assigned Managed Identity is enabled, Python 3.11 Linux Consumption plan is configured, APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING is wired, and App Settings include Key Vault references for TRAPI_ENDPOINT, TRAPI_AUTH_SCOPE, and DATA_STORAGE_ACCOUNT_NAME.
outputs: Updated infra/modules/functionapp.bicep with all required App Settings and Key Vault references
Function App with system-assigned Managed Identity
acceptance_criteria: Function App provisions with system-assigned Managed Identity (principalId emitted as output)
Python 3.11 Linux Consumption plan confirmed
APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING App Setting resolves correctly
TRAPI_ENDPOINT and TRAPI_AUTH_SCOPE App Settings present (Key Vault references or direct values)
DATA_STORAGE_ACCOUNT_NAME App Setting present
No connection strings hardcoded in Bicep or App Settings
risks: Key Vault reference resolution requires Key Vault to be provisioned before Function App references it

tasks.title: Provision Application Insights workspace and Log Analytics via Bicep
tasks.description: Extend or confirm infra/modules/monitoring.bicep to provision a Log Analytics workspace and workspace-based Application Insights instance with 30-day retention and sampling enabled. Wire APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING into Function App App Settings.
outputs: Application Insights with 30-day retention and sampling
Log Analytics workspace
APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING output for functionapp module
acceptance_criteria: Log Analytics workspace provisioned and linked to Application Insights
Retention set to 30 days
Sampling enabled in Application Insights (consistent with host.json samplingSettings)
APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING output available for Function App module
risks: Workspace-based App Insights has different query syntax than classic; alert queries must use workspace tables

tasks.title: Configure RBAC role assignments for Managed Identity via Bicep
tasks.description: Extend infra/modules/rbac.bicep to assign Storage Blob Data Contributor scoped to the yankees-roster container (not account-level) and Key Vault Secrets User scoped to the Key Vault, both targeting the Function App system-assigned Managed Identity principal ID.
inputs: INF-02 (storage account and container resource ID)
outputs: Updated infra/modules/rbac.bicep with container-scoped Storage Blob Data Contributor assignment
Key Vault Secrets User assignment
acceptance_criteria: Storage Blob Data Contributor role assigned to Managed Identity scoped to yankees-roster container (not account-level)
Key Vault Secrets User role assigned to Managed Identity scoped to Key Vault
No owner or contributor roles granted to Function App identity
Role assignments visible in Azure Portal IAM for both resources
risks: Container-scoped RBAC requires container resource ID, not just account ID; Bicep reference must be correct

tasks.title: Store TRAPI credentials in Key Vault and configure App Setting references
tasks.description: If TRAPI requires a client secret or API key (per PRJ-01 outcome), create the secret in Key Vault and configure the Function App App Setting as a Key Vault reference (@Microsoft.KeyVault(...)). If Managed Identity is sufficient, configure the TRAPI auth scope as a plain App Setting.
outputs: TRAPI credentials stored in Key Vault (if applicable)
App Setting TRAPI_ENDPOINT and TRAPI_AUTH_SCOPE configured
Key Vault reference syntax validated in App Settings
acceptance_criteria: No TRAPI credentials appear in source code or App Settings in plaintext
Key Vault reference (if used) resolves successfully — App Setting shows resolved value in portal
Function App can read TRAPI_ENDPOINT and auth scope at runtime without error
risks: Key Vault reference resolution fails if Managed Identity RBAC assignment has not propagated; allow 5-minute delay after INF-05

tasks.title: Author pinned prompt template for 1985 Yankees roster query
tasks.description: Create prompts/get_1985_yankees.txt in source control with a pinned GPT-4o model version directive and a prompt that instructs the model to return the 1985 New York Yankees active roster as structured JSON matching the expected schema. Include system and user message content.
outputs: prompts/get_1985_yankees.txt committed to source control
Pinned GPT-4o model version documented
acceptance_criteria: prompts/get_1985_yankees.txt exists in source control under prompts/
Prompt explicitly requests JSON output matching {players: [{name, position, jersey_number}]} schema
GPT-4o model version is pinned (e.g., gpt-4o-2024-05-13) in the prompt or TRAPI request payload
Prompt produces responses containing Mattingly, Winfield, Henderson in manual test against TRAPI
risks: Model version deprecation causes prompt to use a different model version; pin must be re-validated periodically

tasks.title: Implement TRAPI client with Managed Identity auth, retry, and timeout
tasks.description: Implement or extend trapi_client.py to authenticate to TRAPI using DefaultAzureCredential with the confirmed auth scope, load the prompt template from prompts/get_1985_yankees.txt, call the TRAPI GPT-4o endpoint with a 45-second HTTP timeout, and implement 3 retries with exponential backoff on transient errors.
outputs: Updated trapi_client.py with timeout, retry, and prompt template loading
TRAPI response as parsed JSON dict
acceptance_criteria: HTTP request timeout set to 45 seconds
3 retries with exponential backoff implemented for 429, 500, 502, 503 status codes
DefaultAzureCredential used for bearer token acquisition (no hardcoded credentials)
Prompt template loaded from prompts/get_1985_yankees.txt at runtime
TRAPI response parsed as JSON and returned
Prompt hash (SHA-256) logged on each request for auditability
risks: TRAPI auth scope not confirmed before implementation; auth code may need rework
TRAPI rate limiting may interfere with retry logic; confirm rate limit behavior

tasks.title: Implement response schema validator and player count check
tasks.description: Implement a validator module (src/validator.py or inline in function_app.py) that validates the TRAPI response against the expected JSON schema ({players: [{name, position, jersey_number}]}) and checks that player count is between 24 and 28. Returns a validation result with error details for routing to failed/ prefix.
outputs: src/validator.py (or equivalent module)
ValidationResult with is_valid, player_count, and error_message fields
acceptance_criteria: Validator rejects responses missing players array
Validator rejects responses where any player is missing name, position, or jersey_number
Validator rejects responses with player count < 24 or > 28
Validator accepts responses containing at least Mattingly, Winfield, Henderson
ValidationResult is returned (not raised as exception) to allow failed/ routing
risks: Validation thresholds may need tuning if GPT-4o returns coaching staff or historical variants

tasks.title: Implement blob writer with conditional PUT and failed/ prefix routing
tasks.description: Implement or extend blob_writer.py to write validated roster JSON to yankees-roster/{run_date_utc}.json using DefaultAzureCredential and the dedicated storage account. Implement conditional PUT (If-None-Match: *) to prevent duplicate writes. On validation failure, write raw response to yankees-roster/failed/{run_date_utc}.json.
inputs: INF-02 (storage account name from App Settings)
outputs: Updated blob_writer.py with conditional PUT and failed/ routing
Blob URI logged on success
acceptance_criteria: Validated roster written to yankees-roster/{run_date_utc}.json using UTC date
Conditional PUT (If-None-Match: *) used — second write on same day raises ResourceExistsError (caught and logged, not raised)
Failed validation response written to yankees-roster/failed/{run_date_utc}.json
DefaultAzureCredential used; no connection strings or shared keys
Storage account name read from DATA_STORAGE_ACCOUNT_NAME App Setting
Blob URI emitted as structured log event blob_write_succeeded
risks: Container-scoped RBAC must be confirmed before integration test; SDK may return 403 if role assignment has not propagated

tasks.title: Implement GetAndStoreYankeesRoster Timer Trigger function
tasks.description: Implement function_app.py with the GetAndStoreYankeesRoster Timer Trigger using schedule 0 0 2 * * * (UTC) and useMonitor: true. Orchestrate TRAPI client, validator, and blob writer. Emit structured log events at each stage and raise an alert-triggering exception on validation failure.
outputs: Updated function_app.py with complete Timer Trigger orchestration
Structured log events at all key stages
acceptance_criteria: Timer Trigger schedule set to 0 0 2 * * * (UTC)
useMonitor: true configured in trigger
Structured log events emitted: function_started, trapi_request_sent, trapi_response_received, blob_write_succeeded, function_completed
Validation failure raises exception (surfaced in App Insights as function failure)
Function runs end-to-end locally via func start without errors
risks: Azure Functions v2 Python programming model Timer Trigger useMonitor configuration syntax — verify against SDK docs

tasks.title: Update host.json with functionTimeout and concurrency settings
tasks.description: Update host.json to set functionTimeout to 120 seconds (ISO 8601: 00:02:00), maxConcurrentCalls to 1 for the Timer Trigger, and confirm sampling settings are consistent with Application Insights cost controls.
outputs: Updated host.json with functionTimeout: 00:02:00, extensions.timerTrigger.maxConcurrentCalls: 1
acceptance_criteria: host.json functionTimeout set to 00:02:00
Timer Trigger maxConcurrentCalls set to 1
Sampling settings preserved (isEnabled: true, excludedTypes: Request;Exception)
host.json validates against Azure Functions schema (func start succeeds)
risks: Incorrect ISO 8601 duration format causes silent default; verify via func start output

tasks.title: Validate no secrets in source code; enforce secret scan in CI
tasks.description: Run a secret scan (e.g., GitHub Advanced Security or truffleHog) across the repo to confirm no TRAPI credentials, storage keys, or connection strings are present. Add a secret-scan step to the GitHub Actions CI pipeline to block future PRs that introduce secrets.
inputs: Codebase (all Python files, Bicep files, local.settings.json.example)
outputs: Secret scan passing with zero findings
CI pipeline step blocking secrets on PR
acceptance_criteria: Secret scan reports zero high/critical findings
local.settings.json is in .gitignore and not committed
CI pipeline secret-scan step fails the build if credentials are detected
Key Vault references (not plaintext values) confirmed in App Settings Bicep
risks: Historical git commits may contain secrets; full history scan required, not just HEAD

tasks.title: Implement structured logging with OpenTelemetry SDK and key log events
tasks.description: Integrate the OpenTelemetry SDK (or azure-monitor-opentelemetry) into the Function App to emit structured log events at all key stages: function_started, trapi_request_sent (model version, prompt hash), trapi_response_received (token count, latency ms, player count), blob_write_succeeded (blob URI), function_completed. Add opentelemetry dependencies to requirements-prod.txt.
outputs: Structured log events visible in Application Insights Traces table
Updated requirements-prod.txt with opentelemetry packages
acceptance_criteria: All five key log events appear in Application Insights Traces on each execution
trapi_request_sent includes model version and prompt hash fields
trapi_response_received includes player_count, latency_ms, and token_count fields
blob_write_succeeded includes blob_uri field
Logs queryable in Log Analytics workspace via KQL
risks: OpenTelemetry SDK version conflicts with azure-functions SDK; pin compatible versions and test

tasks.title: Emit player_count_returned custom metric to Application Insights
tasks.description: Emit the player_count_returned custom metric via the Application Insights SDK (or OpenTelemetry metrics API) on each successful run. This metric powers the data quality alert rule and provides trend visibility on GPT output consistency.
outputs: player_count_returned custom metric visible in Application Insights customMetrics table
acceptance_criteria: player_count_returned metric emitted with correct integer value on each successful execution
Metric visible in Application Insights customMetrics table within 2 minutes of execution
Metric not emitted on failed/validation-failure runs (to avoid false data)
risks: Custom metric ingestion latency may delay alert firing; account for 2–5 minute metric pipeline delay in alert evaluation window

tasks.title: Provision Application Insights alert rules via Bicep
tasks.description: Extend infra/modules/alerts.bicep to provision three alert rules: (1) execution failure count > 0 in 1-hour window, (2) function duration > 90 seconds, (3) player_count_returned < 24 or > 40. Configure an email Action Group notifying rciapala for all three alerts.
outputs: Updated infra/modules/alerts.bicep with all three alert rules
Email Action Group for rciapala
acceptance_criteria: Execution failure alert fires within 5 minutes of a deliberate function failure in staging
Duration alert fires when function sleep is injected to exceed 90 seconds in staging
Data quality alert fires when player_count_returned = 10 is emitted in staging test
All alerts notify rciapala via email Action Group
Alert rules provisioned via Bicep (not manually in portal)
risks: Workspace-based App Insights uses different KQL table names (FunctionAppLogs vs traces); alert queries must reference correct tables

tasks.title: Write unit tests for TRAPI client, schema validator, and blob writer
tasks.description: Write pytest unit tests covering: TRAPI client retry/timeout/auth logic (with mocked HTTP responses), schema validator edge cases (missing fields, out-of-range player counts, known player assertions), and blob writer conditional PUT behavior (success, duplicate, failed/ routing). Tests must run in CI without Azure credentials.
outputs: tests/test_trapi_client.py extended with retry/timeout/auth tests
tests/test_validator.py with schema and player count tests
tests/test_blob_writer.py extended with conditional PUT and failed/ routing tests
acceptance_criteria: pytest tests/  passes with zero failures
Coverage >= 80% for trapi_client.py, validator, and blob_writer.py
Tests mock all Azure SDK calls (no live Azure connections required)
Known player assertions (Mattingly, Winfield, Henderson) tested in validator
Conditional PUT duplicate-write scenario tested (ResourceExistsError handled gracefully)
risks: Mocking DefaultAzureCredential token acquisition requires careful patching; use unittest.mock.patch on azure.identity

tasks.title: Write integration test verifying known players appear in blob output
tasks.description: Write an integration test (pytest mark: integration) that calls the real TRAPI endpoint in staging, validates the response, and reads the resulting blob from the staging storage account to assert Mattingly, Winfield, and Henderson are present. Requires staging Azure credentials.
inputs: INF-06 (TRAPI credentials in staging Key Vault)
outputs: tests/test_integration.py with known-player blob assertion
acceptance_criteria: Integration test passes against staging environment
Test asserts blob yankees-roster/{today_utc}.json exists in staging storage account
Test asserts players array contains entries with name=Don Mattingly, Dave Winfield, Rickey Henderson
Test is marked pytest.mark.integration and excluded from unit test CI run
Integration test run documented in CI workflow (manual trigger or staging deployment step)
risks: TRAPI staging availability — test may be flaky if TRAPI staging endpoint has lower SLA

tasks.title: Implement GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline with staging slot deploy and swap
tasks.description: Implement or extend .github/workflows/cd.yml to: (1) run pytest unit tests, (2) deploy to staging slot on push to main, (3) run smoke test against staging slot, (4) swap staging to production on smoke test pass. Use OIDC federation for Azure authentication (no long-lived secrets in GitHub Secrets).
outputs: Updated .github/workflows/cd.yml with full deploy-test-swap pipeline
GitHub OIDC federated identity configured in Azure
acceptance_criteria: Push to main triggers pipeline automatically
Unit tests run and fail the pipeline on test failure
Deployment to staging slot succeeds
Smoke test step validates staging slot responds (timer can be manually triggered or HTTP health check if applicable)
Slot swap to production occurs only after smoke test passes
OIDC federation used — no Azure credentials stored as long-lived GitHub Secrets
risks: Consumption Plan may not support deployment slots; if so, use a separate staging Function App resource

tasks.title: End-to-end staging validation of nightly schedule and blob output
tasks.description: Manually trigger the GetAndStoreYankeesRoster function in the staging environment, verify the blob is written to the staging storage account with correct naming, confirm structured logs appear in Application Insights, and verify no alerts fire spuriously.
outputs: Staging validation sign-off checklist
Application Insights trace showing all key log events
Blob yankees-roster/{date}.json present in staging storage
acceptance_criteria: Function executes successfully in staging within 60 seconds
Blob yankees-roster/{run_date_utc}.json written to staging storage account
All five key log events visible in Application Insights Traces
player_count_returned custom metric emitted with value 24–28
No failure or data quality alerts triggered during valid run
Deliberate failure test confirms failure alert fires within 5 minutes
risks: Cold start on Consumption Plan may delay first execution; allow extra time for staging validation

tasks.title: Write README with local dev setup, TRAPI auth instructions, and blob naming
tasks.description: Update readme.md to include: local development prerequisites (Python 3.11, Azure Functions Core Tools, func start instructions), TRAPI auth setup (how to configure local.settings.json with TRAPI endpoint and auth scope), blob naming convention (yankees-roster/{run_date_utc}.json), and failed/ prefix behavior.
outputs: Updated readme.md with all required sections
acceptance_criteria: README includes local dev setup section with func start instructions
README documents required App Settings and how to populate local.settings.json
README explains TRAPI auth mechanism and how to obtain/configure credentials
README documents blob naming convention and failed/ prefix behavior
README reviewed and approved by rciapala

tasks.title: Production deployment and monitoring activation sign-off
tasks.description: Execute the production deployment via the GitHub Actions pipeline slot swap (triggered by REL-01 on merge to main). Confirm all alert rules are active, Application Insights is receiving data, and the first nightly execution at 2:00 AM UTC is successful.
outputs: Production Function App deployed and running
First nightly execution blob confirmed
All alert rules active and tested in production
acceptance_criteria: Production slot swap completed via GitHub Actions pipeline
First nightly execution at 2:00 AM UTC writes blob successfully
All three alert rules active in production Application Insights
Failure alert test (deliberate exception) fires within 5 minutes in production
All spec success criteria checked off and documented in release notes
risks: First production run is unmonitored overnight; set calendar reminder to verify blob at 2:15 AM UTC on first night
